topic: Paper 2: Texts in Shared Contexts (WWI / Modern Times)
subject: english-literature

items.q: Why does historical context matter when reading literature?
items.correct: Texts emerge from and respond to specific historical conditions — context illuminates references, assumptions and arguments that might otherwise be invisible.
items.misconception: Context just means writing about what the author had for breakfast.
items.random_wrong: Context is required because all texts are about history.
items.why: Students treat context as biographical trivia. Useful context is what the text engages with — politics, ideology, genre conventions — not personal detail of the author's life.

items.correct: A line of five iambs (unstressed-stressed syllable pairs), creating a ten-syllable line with the rhythm da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM da-DUM.
items.misconception: Iambic pentameter is any line with ten syllables.
items.random_wrong: Iambic pentameter is when every line rhymes.
items.why: Syllable count alone doesn't make iambic pentameter — the stress pattern is essential. And iambic pentameter is usually unrhymed (blank verse) in Shakespeare's plays.

items.q: What is the difference between a metaphor and a simile?
items.correct: A simile compares using 'like' or 'as'; a metaphor states one thing IS another, making an implicit comparison without those connectives.
items.misconception: Metaphors are short and similes are long.
items.random_wrong: Metaphors are true and similes are false.
items.why: Length is irrelevant. The defining difference is the use of 'like'/'as' in similes — metaphors omit them, making the comparison stronger and more direct.
It's Brecht. “Beckett” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Who wrote A Streetcar Named Desire?
items.why: Correct answer: Williams. “Miller” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In what year was Waiting for Godot first performed?
items.why: Exact answer: 1953 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
